---
name: project_state
description: Current project state as of 2026-03-21 вҖ” v1 implementation in worktree, pending data model restructure
type: project
---

## Current State (2026-03-21)

**Branch:** `feature/v1-implementation` in worktree `.worktrees/v1-implementation`
**Commits:** 28 implementation commits (13 feat + 15 fix)
**Tests:** 18 passing (queue, reflow, SSE, hevy-sync)

### What works
- Setup flow: API key validation, template selection, queue generation, redirect to Today
- Today page: CARs card, hero session card, Coming Up playlist via SSE
- Session Detail: exercise list with notes, videos, phase coaching
- Progress page: skills, roadmap, benchmarks (static from JSON)
- Content negotiation via Accept header in Datastar @get
- Datastar v1 SSE format (data: elements prefix on every line)
- PWA manifest + service worker (disabled on localhost)

### Blocked: Hevy push/pull
The current data model uses "sessions with inline exercises." Hevy requires Exercise Templates + Routines. A restructure is planned and approved вҖ” see plan file.

### Next step: Data model restructure (approved plan)
**Plan file:** `/Users/ryanr/.claude/plans/jazzy-greeting-hamster.md`

Key changes:
1. Replace `sessions` with `exerciseTemplates` + `routines` in program JSON
2. Exercise templates carry Hevy-compatible fields (type, equipmentCategory, muscleGroup) + our coaching fields (notes, videoURL, tags, progressionByPhase)
3. Routines reference exercise templates by ID, can override sets/notes per context
4. Add `exercise_template_mappings` and `routine_mappings` D1 tables
5. Setup flow creates Hevy exercise templates (for unmatched) + routines upfront
6. Rename session вҶ’ routine throughout codebase
7. Update schema/program.schema.json, types, domain, fragments, router, tests

**Why:** Required for Hevy sync to work. Without this, every push creates duplicate routines and exercises can't be matched.

**How to apply:** Pick up from the plan file. This is the critical path for MVP.

### Datastar v1 gotchas learned
- CDN URL: `https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/gh/starfederation/datastar@1.0.0-RC.8/bundles/datastar.js` (gh/ not npm/)
- Use `data-init` for page load SSE, not `data-on:load`
- Signal names: `data-signals:api-key` becomes `$apiKey` (kebab вҶ’ camelCase)
- `@post` sends signals as JSON body with camelCase keys
- SSE continuation lines need `data: elements ` prefix (with `elements` repeated)
- `executeScript` uses `datastar-patch-elements` with `<script>` tag appended to body
- Service worker caches aggressively вҖ” disable on localhost

     2вҶ’
     3вҶ’## Context
     4вҶ’The current program JSON has "sessions" with inline exercises. Hevy's data model uses Exercise Templates (reusable exercise definitions) and Routines (ordered lists of exercise template references + sets). Adopting this model gives us a correct foundation for Hevy sync, avoids duplicates, and enables future JSON-based program import.
     5вҶ’
     6вҶ’## New Program JSON Structure
     7вҶ’
     8вҶ’### Exercise Templates (top-level array)
     9вҶ’Each exercise defined once. Carries default coaching data. Referenced by routines.
    10вҶ’```json

    12вҶ’  "exerciseTemplates": [
    13вҶ’    {
    14вҶ’      "id": "neck-cars",
    15вҶ’      "title": "Neck CARs",
    16вҶ’
    17вҶ’      // Hevy-compatible fields (used for Hevy sync + custom template creation)
    18вҶ’      "type": "reps_only",                // Hevy enum: duration | reps_only | bodyweight_reps | weight_reps | weight_duration
    19вҶ’      "equipmentCategory": "none",        // Hevy enum: none | resistance_band | dumbbell | barbell | machine | etc.
    20вҶ’      "primaryMuscleGroup": "neck",       // Hevy enum: shoulders | calves | glutes | abdominals | etc.
    21вҶ’      "secondaryMuscleGroups": [],
    22вҶ’
    23вҶ’      // Our coaching fields (not in Hevy вҖ” rendered in routine detail view)
    24вҶ’      "notes": "Slow, controlled circles. Keep shoulders still.",
    25вҶ’      "videoURL": "https://youtube.com/...",
    26вҶ’      "searchTerms": "Neck CARs FRC tutorial",
    27вҶ’      "tags": ["tspine-integration"],           // cross-routine integration tags
    28вҶ’      "progressionByPhase": {                   // per-phase overrides
    29вҶ’        "Foundation": { "sets": "1Г—3 each", "notes": "Reduced range" },
    30вҶ’        "Build": { "sets": "1Г—5 each" }
    31вҶ’      }
    32вҶ’    }
    33вҶ’  ]
    34вҶ’}
    35вҶ’```
    36вҶ’
    37вҶ’### Routines (replaces "sessions")
    38вҶ’Each routine references exercise templates by ID. Can override sets and notes per context.
    39вҶ’```json
    40вҶ’{
    41вҶ’  "routines": [
    42вҶ’    {

    44вҶ’      "title": "Daily CARs",
    45вҶ’      "subtitle": "Controlled Articular Rotations вҖ” 7-10 min",
    46вҶ’      "description": "Do these every single day...",
    47вҶ’      "isDaily": true,
    48вҶ’      "sortOrder": 0,
    49вҶ’      "color": "rgb(96, 165, 96)",
    50вҶ’      "exercises": [
    51вҶ’        {
    52вҶ’          "exerciseTemplateId": "neck-cars",
    53вҶ’          "sets": "1Г—5 each direction",     // required вҖ” the prescription for this routine
    54вҶ’          "notes": "Optional override"      // if omitted, template.notes is used
    55вҶ’        }
    56вҶ’      ]
    57вҶ’    }
    58вҶ’  ]
    59вҶ’}
    60вҶ’```
    61вҶ’
    62вҶ’### Rendering merge rule
    63вҶ’When displaying an exercise in a routine detail view:
    64вҶ’- `sets` вҖ” always from the routine exercise reference
    65вҶ’- `notes` вҖ” routine override if present, else exercise template default
    66вҶ’- `videoURL`, `searchTerms`, `tags`, `progressionByPhase` вҖ” always from exercise template
    67вҶ’- `title`, `type`, `muscleGroup` вҖ” always from exercise template
    68вҶ’
    69вҶ’### Week Templates (unchanged structure)
    70вҶ’Reference routine IDs instead of session IDs:
    71вҶ’```json
    72вҶ’{
    73вҶ’  "weekTemplates": [
    74вҶ’    {

    80вҶ’    }
    81вҶ’  ]
    82вҶ’}
    83вҶ’```
    84вҶ’
    85вҶ’### D1 Tables
    86вҶ’
    87вҶ’**`exercise_template_mappings`** (replaces `exercise_mappings`):
    88вҶ’```sql
    89вҶ’CREATE TABLE exercise_template_mappings (
    90вҶ’  user_id TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES users(id),

    97вҶ’
    98вҶ’**`routine_mappings`** (new вҖ” persists across queue regeneration):
    99вҶ’```sql
   100вҶ’CREATE TABLE routine_mappings (
   101вҶ’  user_id TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES users(id),

   123вҶ’- **`src/domain/hevy-sync.ts`** вҖ” `buildRoutinePayload()`: use `exerciseTemplateId` from routine exercises, look up Hevy template ID from mappings keyed by our template ID (not name).
   124вҶ’

   126вҶ’- **`src/fragments/today.ts`** вҖ” Rename session references to routine.

   130вҶ’
   131вҶ’### Router
   132вҶ’- **`src/index.ts`** вҖ” Rename session references to routine throughout. Update `handleSetup` to create Hevy routines during setup. Update `handlePush` to use pre-created routine IDs. Route `/session/:id` вҶ’ `/routine/:id`.
   133вҶ’
   134вҶ’### Storage
   135вҶ’- **`src/storage/queries.ts`** вҖ” `exercise_mappings` table: change key from `program_exercise_name` to `exercise_template_id`. Add query to bulk-set `hevy_routine_id` by session_id on queue items.
   136вҶ’- **`migrations/0002_rename_exercise_mappings.sql`** вҖ” Migration to update the mappings table.
   137вҶ’
   138вҶ’### Tests
   139вҶ’- **`test/domain/queue.test.ts`** вҖ” Update to use `routines` terminology.

   142вҶ’
   143вҶ’## Setup Flow (with Hevy routine creation)

   145вҶ’1. Validate API key against Hevy

file.content:
# Restructure program data model to match Hevy's Exercise Template + Routine model

## Context
The current program JSON has "sessions" with inline exercises. Hevy's data model uses Exercise Templates (reusable exercise definitions) and Routines (ordered lists of exercise template references + sets). Adopting this model gives us a correct foundation for Hevy sync, avoids duplicates, and enables future JSON-based program import.

## New Program JSON Structure

### Exercise Templates (top-level array)
Each exercise defined once. Carries default coaching data. Referenced by routines.
```json
{
  "exerciseTemplates": [
    {
      "id": "neck-cars",
      "title": "Neck CARs",

      // Hevy-compatible fields (used for Hevy sync + custom template creation)
      "type": "reps_only",                // Hevy enum: duration | reps_only | bodyweight_reps | weight_reps | weight_duration
      "equipmentCategory": "none",        // Hevy enum: none | resistance_band | dumbbell | barbell | machine | etc.
      "primaryMuscleGroup": "neck",       // Hevy enum: shoulders | calves | glutes | abdominals | etc.
      "secondaryMuscleGroups": [],

      // Our coaching fields (not in Hevy вҖ” rendered in routine detail view)
      "notes": "Slow, controlled circles. Keep shoulders still.",
      "videoURL": "https://youtube.com/...",
      "searchTerms": "Neck CARs FRC tutorial",
      "tags": ["tspine-integration"],           // cross-routine integration tags
      "progressionByPhase": {                   // per-phase overrides
        "Foundation": { "sets": "1Г—3 each", "notes": "Reduced range" },
        "Build": { "sets": "1Г—5 each" }
      }
    }
  ]
}
```

### Routines (replaces "sessions")
Each routine references exercise templates by ID. Can override sets and notes per context.
```json
{
  "routines": [
    {
      "id": "daily",
      "title": "Daily CARs",
      "subtitle": "Controlled Articular Rotations вҖ” 7-10 min",
      "description": "Do these every single day...",
      "isDaily": true,
      "sortOrder": 0,
      "color": "rgb(96, 165, 96)",
      "exercises": [
        {
          "exerciseTemplateId": "neck-cars",
          "sets": "1Г—5 each direction",     // required вҖ” the prescription for this routine
          "notes": "Optional override"      // if omitted, template.notes is used
        }
      ]
    }
  ]
}
```

### Rendering merge rule
When displaying an exercise in a routine detail view:
- `sets` вҖ” always from the routine exercise reference
- `notes` вҖ” routine override if present, else exercise template default
- `videoURL`, `searchTerms`, `tags`, `progressionByPhase` вҖ” always from exercise template
- `title`, `type`, `muscleGroup` вҖ” always from exercise template

### Week Templates (unchanged structure)
Reference routine IDs instead of session IDs:
```json
{
  "weekTemplates": [
    {
      "id": "5-day",
      "name": "5-Day (Recommended)",
      "days": [
        { "dayOfWeek": 0, "routineIDs": ["daily", "shoulder-rehab"] }
      ]
    }
  ]
}
```

### D1 Tables

**`exercise_template_mappings`** (replaces `exercise_mappings`):
```sql
CREATE TABLE exercise_template_mappings (
  user_id TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES users(id),
  program_template_id TEXT NOT NULL,     -- our exercise template ID
  hevy_template_id TEXT NOT NULL,        -- Hevy's exercise template ID
  is_custom INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,  -- 1 if we created it in Hevy
  PRIMARY KEY (user_id, program_template_id)
);
```

**`routine_mappings`** (new вҖ” persists across queue regeneration):
```sql
CREATE TABLE routine_mappings (
  user_id TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES users(id),
  program_routine_id TEXT NOT NULL,      -- our routine ID
  hevy_routine_id TEXT NOT NULL,         -- Hevy's routine ID
  PRIMARY KEY (user_id, program_routine_id)
);
```

Queue items still have `hevy_routine_id` but it's populated by looking up `routine_mappings` during queue generation, not during push.

### Other sections unchanged
`progressions`, `roadmap`, `skills`, `benchmarks`, `foundations`, `theme` вҖ” stay as-is.

## Files to modify

### Schema & Data
- **`schema/program.schema.json`** вҖ” Replace `sessions` with `exerciseTemplates` + `routines`. Update `weekTemplates.days[].sessionIDs` вҶ’ `routineIDs`.
- **`programs/mobility-joint-restoration.json`** вҖ” Restructure: extract unique exercises into `exerciseTemplates`, convert sessions to `routines` referencing them.
- **`src/types.ts`** вҖ” Update `Program`, `Session` вҶ’ `Routine`, add `ExerciseTemplate` type. Rename `Session` to `Routine` throughout.

### Domain Logic
- **`src/domain/queue.ts`** вҖ” `generatePlaylist()`: change `sessions` param to `routines`, filter by `isDaily`.
- **`src/domain/reflow.ts`** вҖ” `computeUpcoming()`: `sessions` вҶ’ `routines` parameter.
- **`src/domain/hevy-sync.ts`** вҖ” `buildRoutinePayload()`: use `exerciseTemplateId` from routine exercises, look up Hevy template ID from mappings keyed by our template ID (not name).

### Fragments
- **`src/fragments/today.ts`** вҖ” Rename session references to routine.
- **`src/fragments/session-detail.ts`** вҖ” Rename to `routine-detail.ts`. Look up exercise template details from the `exerciseTemplates` array.
- **`src/fragments/progress.ts`** вҖ” No change (doesn't reference sessions).
- **`src/fragments/setup.ts`** вҖ” No change.

### Router
- **`src/index.ts`** вҖ” Rename session references to routine throughout. Update `handleSetup` to create Hevy routines during setup. Update `handlePush` to use pre-created routine IDs. Route `/session/:id` вҶ’ `/routine/:id`.

### Storage
- **`src/storage/queries.ts`** вҖ” `exercise_mappings` table: change key from `program_exercise_name` to `exercise_template_id`. Add query to bulk-set `hevy_routine_id` by session_id on queue items.
- **`migrations/0002_rename_exercise_mappings.sql`** вҖ” Migration to update the mappings table.

### Tests
- **`test/domain/queue.test.ts`** вҖ” Update to use `routines` terminology.
- **`test/domain/reflow.test.ts`** вҖ” Same.
- **`test/domain/hevy-sync.test.ts`** вҖ” Update to use exercise template IDs.

## Setup Flow (with Hevy routine creation)

1. Validate API key against Hevy
2. Fetch all Hevy exercise templates
3. Auto-match program `exerciseTemplates` to Hevy templates by name
4. For unmatched: create custom exercise templates in Hevy via API
5. Save all mappings (program template ID вҶ’ Hevy template ID) to D1
6. For each routine in program: build Hevy routine payload, create via API, store Hevy routine ID
7. Create user + generate queue + set hevy_routine_id on queue items
8. Redirect to Today

## Verification
1. Reset D1
2. Enter API key, click 5-Day
3. `get-routines` via Hevy MCP вҶ’ should show 6 routines
4. `search-exercise-templates` вҶ’ should show custom templates for exercises Hevy didn't have
5. Today page renders with Push to Hevy buttons
6. All 18 tests pass (updated)
7. D1: `SELECT hevy_routine_id FROM queue_items LIMIT 5` вҶ’ all have routine IDs

Base directory for this skill: /Users/ryanr/.claude/plugins/cache/claude-plugins-official/superpowers/5.0.5/skills/executing-plans

# Executing Plans

## Overview

Load plan, review critically, execute all tasks, report when complete.

**Announce at start:** "I'm using the executing-plans skill to implement this plan."

**Note:** Tell your human partner that Superpowers works much better with access to subagents. The quality of its work will be significantly higher if run on a platform with subagent support (such as Claude Code or Codex). If subagents are available, use superpowers:subagent-driven-development instead of this skill.

## The Process

### Step 1: Load and Review Plan
1. Read plan file
2. Review critically - identify any questions or concerns about the plan
3. If concerns: Raise them with your human partner before starting
4. If no concerns: Create TodoWrite and proceed

### Step 2: Execute Tasks

For each task:
1. Mark as in_progress
2. Follow each step exactly (plan has bite-sized steps)
3. Run verifications as specified
4. Mark as completed

### Step 3: Complete Development

After all tasks complete and verified:
- Announce: "I'm using the finishing-a-development-branch skill to complete this work."
- **REQUIRED SUB-SKILL:** Use superpowers:finishing-a-development-branch
- Follow that skill to verify tests, present options, execute choice

## When to Stop and Ask for Help

**STOP executing immediately when:**
- Hit a blocker (missing dependency, test fails, instruction unclear)
- Plan has critical gaps preventing starting
- You don't understand an instruction
- Verification fails repeatedly

**Ask for clarification rather than guessing.**

## When to Revisit Earlier Steps

**Return to Review (Step 1) when:**
- Partner updates the plan based on your feedback
- Fundamental approach needs rethinking

**Don't force through blockers** - stop and ask.

## Remember
- Review plan critically first
- Follow plan steps exactly
- Don't skip verifications
- Reference skills when plan says to
- Stop when blocked, don't guess
- Never start implementation on main/master branch without explicit user consent

## Integration

**Required workflow skills:**
- **superpowers:using-git-worktrees** - REQUIRED: Set up isolated workspace before starting
- **superpowers:writing-plans** - Creates the plan this skill executes

1вҶ’import type { UserRow, QueueItemRow, ExerciseMappingRow } from "../types";
     2вҶ’
     3вҶ’export async function getUser(db: D1Database, userId: string): Promise<UserRow | null> {
     4вҶ’  return db.prepare("SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = ?").bind(userId).first<UserRow>();
     5вҶ’}
     6вҶ’
     7вҶ’export async function upsertUser(
     8вҶ’  db: D1Database,
     9вҶ’  user: { id: string; active_program: string; template_id: string; start_date: string; hevy_api_key?: string }
    10вҶ’): Promise<void> {
    11вҶ’  await db
    12вҶ’    .prepare(
    13вҶ’      `INSERT INTO users (id, active_program, template_id, start_date, hevy_api_key)
    14вҶ’       VALUES (?, ?, ?, ?, ?)
    15вҶ’       ON CONFLICT(id) DO UPDATE SET
    16вҶ’         active_program = excluded.active_program,
    17вҶ’         template_id = excluded.template_id,
    18вҶ’         start_date = excluded.start_date,
    19вҶ’         hevy_api_key = COALESCE(excluded.hevy_api_key, users.hevy_api_key)`
    20вҶ’    )
    21вҶ’    .bind(user.id, user.active_program, user.template_id, user.start_date, user.hevy_api_key ?? null)
    22вҶ’    .run();
    23вҶ’}
    24вҶ’
    25вҶ’export async function getQueueItems(db: D1Database, userId: string): Promise<QueueItemRow[]> {
    26вҶ’  const result = await db
    27вҶ’    .prepare("SELECT * FROM queue_items WHERE user_id = ? ORDER BY position")
    28вҶ’    .bind(userId)
    29вҶ’    .all<QueueItemRow>();
    30вҶ’  return result.results;
    31вҶ’}
    32вҶ’
    33вҶ’export async function insertQueueItems(
    34вҶ’  db: D1Database,
    35вҶ’  userId: string,
    36вҶ’  items: Array<{ session_id: string; position: number }>
    37вҶ’): Promise<void> {
    38вҶ’  const stmt = db.prepare(
    39вҶ’    "INSERT INTO queue_items (user_id, session_id, position) VALUES (?, ?, ?)"
    40вҶ’  );
    41вҶ’  const batch = items.map((item) => stmt.bind(userId, item.session_id, item.position));
    42вҶ’  await db.batch(batch);
    43вҶ’}
    44вҶ’
    45вҶ’export async function markQueueItemCompleted(
    46вҶ’  db: D1Database,
    47вҶ’  itemId: number,
    48вҶ’  completedDate: string,
    49вҶ’  hevyWorkoutId?: string
    50вҶ’): Promise<void> {
    51вҶ’  await db
    52вҶ’    .prepare(
    53вҶ’      "UPDATE queue_items SET status = 'completed', completed_date = ?, hevy_workout_id = ? WHERE id = ?"
    54вҶ’    )
    55вҶ’    .bind(completedDate, hevyWorkoutId ?? null, itemId)
    56вҶ’    .run();
    57вҶ’}
    58вҶ’
    59вҶ’export async function markQueueItemCompletedForUser(
    60вҶ’  db: D1Database,
    61вҶ’  itemId: number,
    62вҶ’  userId: string,
    63вҶ’  completedDate: string,
    64вҶ’  hevyWorkoutId?: string
    65вҶ’): Promise<void> {
    66вҶ’  await db
    67вҶ’    .prepare(
    68вҶ’      "UPDATE queue_items SET status = 'completed', completed_date = ?, hevy_workout_id = ? WHERE id = ? AND user_id = ?"
    69вҶ’    )
    70вҶ’    .bind(completedDate, hevyWorkoutId ?? null, itemId, userId)
    71вҶ’    .run();
    72вҶ’}
    73вҶ’
    74вҶ’export async function updateQueueItemHevyRoutineId(
    75вҶ’  db: D1Database,
    76вҶ’  itemId: number,
    77вҶ’  routineId: string
    78вҶ’): Promise<void> {
    79вҶ’  await db
    80вҶ’    .prepare("UPDATE queue_items SET hevy_routine_id = ? WHERE id = ?")
    81вҶ’    .bind(routineId, itemId)
    82вҶ’    .run();
    83вҶ’}
    84вҶ’
    85вҶ’export async function getExerciseMappings(
    86вҶ’  db: D1Database,
    87вҶ’  userId: string
    88вҶ’): Promise<ExerciseMappingRow[]> {
    89вҶ’  const result = await db
    90вҶ’    .prepare("SELECT * FROM exercise_mappings WHERE user_id = ?")
    91вҶ’    .bind(userId)
    92вҶ’    .all<ExerciseMappingRow>();
    93вҶ’  return result.results;
    94вҶ’}
    95вҶ’
    96вҶ’export async function upsertExerciseMapping(
    97вҶ’  db: D1Database,
    98вҶ’  mapping: ExerciseMappingRow
    99вҶ’): Promise<void> {
   100вҶ’  await db
   101вҶ’    .prepare(
   102вҶ’      `INSERT INTO exercise_mappings (user_id, program_exercise_name, hevy_exercise_id, confirmed_by_user)
   103вҶ’       VALUES (?, ?, ?, ?)
   104вҶ’       ON CONFLICT(user_id, program_exercise_name) DO UPDATE SET
   105вҶ’         hevy_exercise_id = excluded.hevy_exercise_id,
   106вҶ’         confirmed_by_user = excluded.confirmed_by_user`
   107вҶ’    )
   108вҶ’    .bind(mapping.user_id, mapping.program_exercise_name, mapping.hevy_exercise_id, mapping.confirmed_by_user)
   109вҶ’    .run();
   110вҶ’}
   111вҶ’

    58вҶ’          "notes": "On all fours, roll through flexion and extension one vertebra at a time. Don't rush. This decompresses the spine after desk work.",

This directly unlocks the thoracic extension your shoulders need for overhead pressing and front rack position.",

The rotation should come from your mid-back, NOT your lower back вҖ” keeping your knees stacked prevents lumbar rotation. This is the movement pattern that lets you actually rotate during daily life without shoulder or low back compensation.",

{
  "meta": {
    "version": "1.0.0",
    "title": "Hevy Planner",
    "subtitle": "Personalized Training Protocol",
    "description": "8-week joint restoration program with 12-month skills roadmap. Rebuilds shoulder stability, knee resilience, Achilles health, core strength, and thoracic mobility вҖ” then progresses to muscle-ups, tuck planche, pistol squats, and handstands.",
    "author": "AI Coach",
    "durationWeeks": 0,
    "tags": ["mobility", "joint-health", "rehabilitation", "calisthenics", "skills"]
  },

  "sessions": [
    {
      "id": "daily",
      "title": "Daily CARs",
      "subtitle": "Controlled Articular Rotations вҖ” 7-10 min",
      "description": "Do these every single day, no exceptions. CARs are the foundation вҖ” they maintain joint health, identify restrictions early, and prime your nervous system. Think of them as brushing your teeth for your joints.",
      "isDaily": true,
      "sortOrder": 1,
      "color": "rgb(96, 165, 224)",
      "exercises": [
        {
          "id": "neck-cars",
          "name": "Neck CARs",

file.content:
{
  "meta": {
    "version": "1.0.0",
    "title": "Hevy Planner",
    "subtitle": "Personalized Training Protocol",
    "description": "8-week joint restoration program with 12-month skills roadmap. Rebuilds shoulder stability, knee resilience, Achilles health, core strength, and thoracic mobility вҖ” then progresses to muscle-ups, tuck planche, pistol squats, and handstands.",
    "author": "AI Coach",
    "durationWeeks": 0,
    "tags": ["mobility", "joint-health", "rehabilitation", "calisthenics", "skills"]
  },

  "sessions": [
    {
      "id": "daily",
      "title": "Daily CARs",
      "subtitle": "Controlled Articular Rotations вҖ” 7-10 min",
      "description": "Do these every single day, no exceptions. CARs are the foundation вҖ” they maintain joint health, identify restrictions early, and prime your nervous system. Think of them as brushing your teeth for your joints.",
      "isDaily": true,
      "sortOrder": 1,
      "color": "rgb(96, 165, 224)",
      "exercises": [
        {
          "id": "neck-cars",
          "name": "Neck CARs",
          "sets": "1Г—5 each direction",
          "notes": "Slow, controlled circles. Keep shoulders still. If you find a sticky spot, spend extra time there.",
          "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=neck+CARs+controlled+articular+rotations+tutorial",
          "searchTerms": "Neck CARs FRC tutorial"
        },
        {
          "id": "shoulder-cars",
          "name": "Shoulder CARs",
          "sets": "1Г—5 each direction, each arm",
          "notes": "CRITICAL for you. Make fist, lock elbow straight, trace the biggest circle possible. Go SLOW вҖ” 10 seconds per rotation minimum. Pain-free range only, expand it over weeks.",
          "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=shoulder+CARs+controlled+articular+rotations",
          "searchTerms": "Shoulder CARs FRC"
        },
        {
          "id": "hip-cars",
          "name": "Hip CARs (Standing)",
          "sets": "1Г—5 each direction, each leg",
          "notes": "Hold a wall for balance. Drive knee up, open out, extend back, return. Keep pelvis still вҖ” the rotation happens at the hip socket only.",
          "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=standing+hip+CARs+FRC+tutorial",
          "searchTerms": "Standing hip CARs FRC"
        },
        {
          "id": "ankle-cars",
          "name": "Ankle CARs",
          "sets": "1Г—5 each direction, each foot",
          "notes": "CRITICAL вҖ” especially right side (Achilles repair). Seated, lift foot off ground, make slow full circles. Note any clicking or restriction without forcing through it.",
          "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=ankle+CARs+controlled+articular+rotations",
          "searchTerms": "Ankle CARs FRC"
        },
        {
          "id": "spine-cars",
          "name": "Spine CARs (Segmental Cat-Cow)",
          "sets": "1Г—8 slow reps",
          "notes": "On all fours, roll through flexion and extension one vertebra at a time. Don't rush. This decompresses the spine after desk work.",
          "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=segmental+cat+cow+spine+CARs",
          "searchTerms": "Segmental cat-cow spine CARs"
        },
        {
          "id": "wrist-cars",
          "name": "Wrist CARs",
          "sets": "1Г—5 each direction, each wrist",
          "notes": "Make a fist, trace slow full circles at the wrist. You're doing planks, bear crawls, and bench pressing вҖ” your wrists take a beating. These also help you identify early warning signs of overuse before they become problems.",
          "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=wrist+CARs+controlled+articular+rotations",
          "searchTerms": "Wrist CARs FRC"
        },
        {
          "id": "tspine-rotation-quadruped",
          "name": "T-Spine Rotation (Quadruped)",
          "sets": "1Г—5 each side",
          "notes": "On all fours, place one hand behind your head. Rotate that elbow toward the ceiling, then down toward the opposite wrist. Follow your elbow with your eyes. This is the single most important addition for you вҖ” your desk-locked thoracic spine is the upstream bottleneck for your shoulders. If this doesn't rotate, your shoulder compensates during every press.",
          "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=quadruped+thoracic+rotation+mobility+drill",
          "searchTerms": "Quadruped thoracic rotation"
        }
      ]
    },
    {
      "id": "shoulder-rehab",
      "title": "Session A: Shoulder Rehab & Stability",
      "subtitle": "15-20 min вҖ” Do 2x per week",
      "description": "Your shoulders are compensating for weak scapular stabilizers and rotator cuff. This session rebuilds the foundation so your pressing strength can actually express itself without pain. Within 3-4 weeks, planks and overhead work should start feeling noticeably better.",
      "sortOrder": 2,
      "color": "rgb(224, 134, 96)",
      "exercises": [
        {
          "id": "dead-hangs",
          "name": "Dead Hangs",
          "sets": "3Г—20-30 sec",
          "notes": "Use your pull-up bar. Passive hang, let your shoulders stretch toward your ears. This decompresses the shoulder joint and builds grip. If 30s is easy, progress to active hangs (pull shoulders down while hanging).",
          "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=dead+hang+shoulder+mobility+progression",
          "searchTerms": "Dead hang shoulder health"
        },
        {
          "id": "tspine-extension-foam-roller",
          "name": "T-Spine Extension Over Foam Roller",
          "sets": "2Г—10 reps + 30 sec hold",
          "notes": "Place foam roller across your mid-back. Support your head with hands, elbows pointed forward. Extend back over the roller, then crunch forward вҖ” 10 reps. Then hold the extended position for 30 seconds. Move the roller up or down one vertebra and repeat. This directly unlocks the thoracic extension your shoulders need for overhead pressing and front rack position.",
          "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=thoracic+extension+foam+roller+mobility",
          "searchTerms": "T-spine extension foam roller",
          "tags": ["tspine-integration"]
        },
        {
          "id": "open-book",
          "name": "Open Book (Thread the Needle)",
          "sets": "2Г—8 each side",
          "notes": "Lie on your side, knees stacked and bent at 90В°. Top arm opens toward the ceiling and behind you while keeping knees together. Follow your hand with your eyes. The rotation should come from your mid-back, NOT your lower back вҖ” keeping your knees stacked prevents lumbar rotation. This is the movement pattern that lets you actually rotate during daily life without shoulder or low back compensation.",
          "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=open+book+thoracic+rotation+mobility+drill",
          "searchTerms": "Open book thoracic rotation",
          "tags": ["tspine-integration"]
        },
        {
          "id": "scapular-pushups",
          "name": "Scapular Push-ups",
          "sets": "3Г—10",
          "notes": "Plank position, arms locked straight. Only your shoulder blades move вҖ” protract (spread apart) and retract (squeeze together). This trains serratus anterior, which is likely weak and causing your plank shoulder pain.",
          "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=scapular+push+ups+tutorial+serratus",
          "searchTerms": "Scapular push-ups serratus"
        },
        {
          "id": "banded-external-rotation",
          "name": "Banded External Rotation at 90В°",
          "sets": "3Г—12 each side",
          "notes": "Elbow at 90В°, upper arm at shoulder height. Rotate forearm up against band resistance. Slow eccentric (3 sec lowering). This directly strengthens the rotator cuff in the position where it fails during pressing.",
          "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=banded+external+rotation+90+degrees+rotator+cuff",
          "searchTerms": "Banded external rotation 90 degrees"
        },
        {
          "id": "prone-ytw-raises",
          "name": "Prone Y-T-W Raises",
          "sets": "2Г—8 each position",
          "notes": "Lie face down on bench, light dumbbells (3-5 lb to start). Y = arms overhead in a V. T = arms straight out. W = elbows bent, squeeze shoulder blades. If 5 lb is too much, start bodyweight. These are humbling.",
          "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=prone+Y+T+W+raises+shoulder+rehab",
          "searchTerms": "Prone Y-T-W raises"
        },
        {
          "id": "wall-slides",
          "name": "Wall Slides",
          "sets": "3Г—10",
          "notes": "Back and arms flat against wall. Slide arms up overhead keeping everything in contact with the wall. If your lower back arches off the wall, that's a mobility restriction вҖ” only go as high as you can maintain contact.",
          "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=wall+slides+shoulder+mobility+exercise",
          "searchTerms": "Wall slides shoulder mobility"
        },
        {
          "id": "shoulder-sleeper-stretch",
          "name": "Shoulder Sleeper Stretch with PAILs/RAILs",
          "sets": "2 min each side",
          "notes": "Lie on your side, bottom arm at 90В°. Use top hand to gently press into internal rotation. Hold 60s passive. Then PAILs: push bottom hand INTO the floor (10s, 20% effort building to 70%). Then RAILs: try to rotate hand UP without moving (10s same ramp). Re-stretch вҖ” you'll gain range.",
          "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=shoulder+PAILs+RAILs+sleeper+stretch+FRC",
          "searchTerms": "PAILs RAILs shoulder sleeper stretch"
        },
        {
          "id": "hollow-body-hold-a",
          "name": "Hollow Body Hold",
          "sets": "3Г—20-30 sec",
          "notes": "Lie on your back, arms overhead, legs extended. Press lower back INTO the floor and lift shoulders and feet slightly off the ground. If your low back peels off the floor, bend your knees or bring arms to your sides to regress. This is the missing link behind your plank shoulder pain вҖ” when your core can't hold position, your shoulders compensate. Build this, and planks and pressing improve.",
          "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=hollow+body+hold+tutorial+progression",
          "searchTerms": "Hollow body hold progression",
          "tags": ["core-integration"]
        }
      ]
    },
    {
      "id": "knee-hip-mobility",
      "title": "Session B: Knee & Hip Mobility",
      "subtitle": "15-20 min вҖ” Do 2x per week",
      "description": "Your lateral lunge pain and cross-legged sitting difficulty both point to tight hip adductors and weak VMO (inner quad). This session opens the hips and builds the knee stability that lets you load lateral movements safely. The Cossack squat is your eventual goal movement.",
      "sortOrder": 3,
      "color": "rgb(96, 200, 140)",
      "exercises": [
        {
          "id": "ninety-ninety-hip-switches",
          "name": "90/90 Hip Switches",
          "sets": "3Г—8 each side",
          "notes": "Sit with both legs at 90В°. Rotate from one side to the other. Keep spine tall. If you can't get your knees to the floor, that's fine вҖ” work the range you have. This is your hip mobility barometer.",
          "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=90+90+hip+switches+mobility+tutorial",
          "searchTerms": "90/90 hip switches mobility"
        },
        {
          "id": "glute-bridge",
          "name": "Glute Bridge with 3-sec Hold",
          "sets": "3Г—12",
          "notes": "Lie on back, knees bent, feet flat. Drive through heels, squeeze glutes HARD at the top, hold 3 seconds. Lower slowly. If you feel this in your hamstrings more than your glutes, bring your feet closer to your butt. This wakes up the glutes so they fire properly during squats, lunges, and walking. At 46 with desk work, your glutes are almost certainly underperforming вҖ” this fixes that.",
          "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=glute+bridge+hold+activation+tutorial",
          "searchTerms": "Glute bridge activation hold",
          "tags": ["glute-activation"]
        },
        {
          "id": "banded-clamshells",
          "name": "Banded Clamshells",
          "sets": "3Г—15 each side",
          "notes": "Lie on your side, knees bent, band around knees. Open top knee against band resistance while keeping feet together. Slow and controlled вҖ” don't let the band snap your knee back down. This targets the gluteus medius, which stabilizes your pelvis and directly protects your knees during lateral movements. If your Cossack squats and side lunges hurt, weak glute med is likely a big contributor.",
          "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=banded+clamshell+glute+medius+activation",
          "searchTerms": "Banded clamshell glute medius",
          "tags": ["glute-activation"]
        },
        {
          "id": "tibialis-raises",

content.content:
200вҶ’          "id": "tibialis-raises",
   201вҶ’          "name": "Tibialis Anterior Raises",
   202вҶ’          "sets": "3Г—15",
   203вҶ’          "notes": "Stand with back against wall, feet ~12 inches out. Pull toes up toward shins, lower slowly. This strengthens the front of your shin and protects the knee. The Knees Over Toes Guy made this famous for good reason.",
   204вҶ’          "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=tibialis+raises+knees+over+toes+tutorial",
   205вҶ’          "searchTerms": "Tibialis raises Knees Over Toes"
   206вҶ’        },
   207вҶ’        {
   208вҶ’          "id": "banded-tke",
   209вҶ’          "name": "Banded Terminal Knee Extensions",
   210вҶ’          "sets": "3Г—15 each leg",
   211вҶ’          "notes": "Attach band behind your knee to a low anchor. Stand facing away, slight bend. Squeeze to full lockout against band resistance. This isolates the VMO вҖ” the inner quad muscle that stabilizes the kneecap.",
   212вҶ’          "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=banded+terminal+knee+extension+VMO",

   215вҶ’        {
   216вҶ’          "id": "atg-split-squat",
   217вҶ’          "name": "ATG Split Squat (Regressed)",
   218вҶ’          "sets": "3Г—8 each leg",
   219вҶ’          "notes": "Start with hands on bench/chair for support. Back knee tracks all the way down and forward. ONLY go as deep as you can control without pain. Weeks 1-2: use support. Weeks 3-4: reduce support. This is the single best knee bulletproofing exercise.",
   220вҶ’          "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=ATG+split+squat+regression+beginner+knees+over+toes",
   221вҶ’          "searchTerms": "ATG split squat beginner regression",
   222вҶ’          "progressionByPhase": {
   223вҶ’            "Foundation": { "sets": "3Г—8 each leg", "notes": "Use bench support, partial depth only." },
   224вҶ’            "Build": { "sets": "3Г—8 each leg", "notes": "Reduce bench support, increase depth." },
   225вҶ’            "Load": { "sets": "3Г—8 each leg", "notes": "Light dumbbells, no support." }
   226вҶ’          }
   227вҶ’        },
   228вҶ’        {
   229вҶ’          "id": "cossack-squat",
   230вҶ’          "name": "Cossack Squat (Partial ROM)",
   231вҶ’          "sets": "3Г—6 each side",
   232вҶ’          "notes": "Bodyweight only. Start with a small range вҖ” you do NOT need to go ass-to-grass. Hold a doorframe or rack for balance. This directly trains the lateral movement pattern that's giving your knees trouble. Depth will come with consistency.",
   233вҶ’          "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=cossack+squat+beginner+progression+mobility",
   234вҶ’          "searchTerms": "Cossack squat progression beginner",
   235вҶ’          "progressionByPhase": {
   236вҶ’            "Foundation": { "sets": "3Г—6 each side", "notes": "Hold doorframe, go only as deep as comfortable." },
   237вҶ’            "Build": { "sets": "3Г—6 each side", "notes": "Less support, aim for parallel thigh depth." },
   238вҶ’            "Load": { "sets": "3Г—6 each side", "notes": "Goblet Cossack Squats with light dumbbell." }
   239вҶ’          }
   240вҶ’        },
   241вҶ’        {
   242вҶ’          "id": "active-frog-stretch",
   243вҶ’          "name": "Active Frog Stretch",
   244вҶ’          "sets": "2Г—45 sec",
   245вҶ’          "notes": "On all fours, knees wide, rock forward and back. Keep spine neutral. This opens the adductors (inner thigh) which are contributing to both your lateral lunge pain and cross-legged sitting difficulty. Active rocking > static hold.",
   246вҶ’          "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=active+frog+stretch+hip+adductor+mobility",
   247вҶ’          "searchTerms": "Active frog stretch adductors"
   248вҶ’        },
   249вҶ’        {
   250вҶ’          "id": "copenhagen-plank-b",
   251вҶ’          "name": "Copenhagen Plank",
   252вҶ’          "sets": "3Г—15-20 sec each side",
   253вҶ’          "notes": "Side plank position with top leg on a bench, bottom leg hanging free. This is a two-for-one вҖ” it trains lateral core strength AND adductor strength simultaneously. Directly addresses your lateral lunge knee pain. Regress by using bottom knee on the floor or shortening the hold. These are brutally hard at first вҖ” 10 seconds is fine to start.",
   254вҶ’          "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=copenhagen+plank+tutorial+regression+progression",
   255вҶ’          "searchTerms": "Copenhagen plank tutorial progression",
   256вҶ’          "tags": ["core-integration"]
   257вҶ’        }
   258вҶ’      ]
   259вҶ’    },
   260вҶ’    {
   261вҶ’      "id": "ankle-integration",
   262вҶ’      "title": "Session C: Ankle/Achilles & Integration",
   263вҶ’      "subtitle": "15-20 min вҖ” Do 1-2x per week",
   264вҶ’      "description": "Your repaired Achilles needs consistent eccentric loading to stay resilient вҖ” this is backed by decades of research (Alfredson protocol). Combined with dorsiflexion work, this session protects the ankle and unlocks deeper squat positions. The balance work re-trains proprioception that was disrupted by the surgery.",
   265вҶ’      "sortOrder": 4,
   266вҶ’      "color": "rgb(180, 140, 224)",
   267вҶ’      "exercises": [
   268вҶ’        {
   269вҶ’          "id": "extended-ankle-cars",
   270вҶ’          "name": "Extended Ankle CARs",
   271вҶ’          "sets": "2Г—8 each direction, each foot",
   272вҶ’          "notes": "Slower and more deliberate than your daily CARs. Spend 5+ seconds per rotation on the right ankle. Map out the full range, note where it catches. Over weeks, the circle should get bigger.",
   273вҶ’          "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=ankle+CARs+FRC+detailed+tutorial",
   274вҶ’          "searchTerms": "Ankle CARs detailed FRC"
   275вҶ’        },
   276вҶ’        {
   277вҶ’          "id": "eccentric-calf-raises",
   278вҶ’          "name": "Eccentric Calf Raises (Alfredson Protocol)",
   279вҶ’          "sets": "3Г—15 each leg",
   280вҶ’          "notes": "CRITICAL for your Achilles. Stand on step edge, raise up on BOTH feet, then lower slowly (3-4 sec) on ONE foot. Do both straight-knee (gastroc) and bent-knee (soleus) versions. The right side may feel tight вҖ” stay in a pain-free range. This is the gold standard for Achilles tendon health.",
   281вҶ’          "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=eccentric+calf+raises+achilles+alfredson+protocol",
   282вҶ’          "searchTerms": "Eccentric calf raises Alfredson Achilles",
   283вҶ’          "progressionByPhase": {
   284вҶ’            "Foundation": { "notes": "Both-leg lower if single-leg is too intense on the right." },
   285вҶ’            "Build": { "notes": "Full single-leg protocol." }
   286вҶ’          }
   287вҶ’        },
   288вҶ’        {
   289вҶ’          "id": "wall-dorsiflexion",
   290вҶ’          "name": "Wall Dorsiflexion Mobilization",
   291вҶ’          "sets": "3Г—30 sec each side",
   292вҶ’          "notes": "Face wall, foot ~4 inches away. Drive knee forward over toes, keeping heel DOWN. Measure progress by how far from the wall you can get your foot while still touching knee to wall. Your right side will be tighter вҖ” that's the Achilles repair limiting dorsiflexion.",
   293вҶ’          "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=wall+ankle+dorsiflexion+mobilization+test",
   294вҶ’          "searchTerms": "Wall dorsiflexion ankle mobilization"
   295вҶ’        },
   296вҶ’        {
   297вҶ’          "id": "single-leg-balance",
   298вҶ’          "name": "Single-Leg Balance (Eyes Closed)",
   299вҶ’          "sets": "3Г—30 sec each leg",
   300вҶ’          "notes": "Start with eyes open if needed. Bare feet on firm surface. The goal is rebuilding proprioception in the repaired ankle. If 30s is easy with eyes closed, try it on a folded towel for an unstable surface.",
   301вҶ’          "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=single+leg+balance+proprioception+ankle+rehab",
   302вҶ’          "searchTerms": "Single leg balance proprioception",
   303вҶ’          "progressionByPhase": {
   304вҶ’            "Load": { "notes": "Single-leg balance on unstable surface (folded towel or pillow)." }
   305вҶ’          }
   306вҶ’        },
   307вҶ’        {
   308вҶ’          "id": "supported-deep-squat",
   309вҶ’          "name": "Supported Deep Squat Hold",
   310вҶ’          "sets": "3Г—30-45 sec",
   311вҶ’          "notes": "Hold a doorframe, squat rack, or heavy dumbbell as counterbalance. Sink as deep as comfortable, heels down. Let your hips open and spine lengthen. This integrates ankle, knee, and hip mobility into one functional position. If heels lift, elevate them on a plate or book.",
   312вҶ’          "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=deep+squat+hold+mobility+assisted+progression",
   313вҶ’          "searchTerms": "Deep squat hold assisted mobility",
   314вҶ’          "progressionByPhase": {
   315вҶ’            "Load": { "notes": "Weighted deep squat holds (goblet position)." }
   316вҶ’          }
   317вҶ’        },
   318вҶ’        {
   319вҶ’          "id": "dead-bugs-c",
   320вҶ’          "name": "Dead Bugs",
   321вҶ’          "sets": "3Г—8 each side",
   322вҶ’          "notes": "Lie on your back, arms up, knees at 90В°. Press lower back into floor. Slowly extend opposite arm and leg while maintaining ZERO low back movement. If your back arches, you've gone too far вҖ” shorten the range. This trains anti-extension and hip dissociation, directly improving your squat and overhead positions.",
   323вҶ’          "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=dead+bug+exercise+tutorial+anti+extension",
   324вҶ’          "searchTerms": "Dead bug exercise anti-extension",
   325вҶ’          "tags": ["core-integration"]
   326вҶ’        },
   327вҶ’        {
   328вҶ’          "id": "bear-crawl",
   329вҶ’          "name": "Bear Crawl",
   330вҶ’          "sets": "2Г—30 sec",
   331вҶ’          "notes": "Hands and feet on ground, knees hovering ~2 inches off floor. Crawl forward and backward slowly. This integrates shoulder stability, hip mobility, and ankle mobility under load in a way that mimics real-world movement. Keep breathing.",
   332вҶ’          "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=bear+crawl+exercise+mobility+tutorial",
   333вҶ’          "searchTerms": "Bear crawl exercise mobility"
   334вҶ’        }
   335вҶ’      ]
   336вҶ’    },
   337вҶ’    {
   338вҶ’      "id": "core-stability",
   339вҶ’      "title": "Session D: Core Stability",
   340вҶ’      "subtitle": "15-20 min вҖ” Do 1-2x per week",
   341вҶ’      "description": "Your shoulders, knees, and ankles all depend on a stable trunk. When the core can't hold position, your joints compensate вҖ” that's a big part of why you keep getting injured. This session trains anti-extension, anti-rotation, and anti-lateral-flexion вҖ” the three pillars of functional core stability. These aren't crunches; they teach your core to resist unwanted movement so your limbs can move freely. Exercises marked with \u25c6 in other sessions are drawn from here.",
   342вҶ’      "sortOrder": 5,
   343вҶ’      "color": "rgb(224, 120, 150)",
   344вҶ’      "exercises": [
   345вҶ’        {
   346вҶ’          "id": "dead-bugs-d",
   347вҶ’          "name": "Dead Bugs",
   348вҶ’          "sets": "3Г—10 each side",
   349вҶ’          "notes": "The king of core exercises for your situation. Lie on back, arms up, knees at 90В°. Press lower back into floor вҖ” this is the cue that matters. Extend opposite arm and leg while maintaining ZERO low back movement. Slow and controlled вҖ” 3 seconds out, 3 seconds back. If your back arches, shorten the range. Progress by holding a light dumbbell overhead or adding a band around your feet.",
   350вҶ’          "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=dead+bug+exercise+tutorial+progression",
   351вҶ’          "searchTerms": "Dead bug exercise progression"
   352вҶ’        },
   353вҶ’        {
   354вҶ’          "id": "pallof-press",
   355вҶ’          "name": "Pallof Press",
   356вҶ’          "sets": "3Г—10 each side",
   357вҶ’          "notes": "Attach band to anchor at chest height. Stand sideways, hold band at chest. Press straight out and hold 2 seconds. The band tries to rotate you вҖ” your core resists. This anti-rotation strength directly supports your Cossack squats and lateral lunge stability. Keep hips square, don't let the band pull you.",
   358вҶ’          "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=pallof+press+band+anti+rotation+core",
   359вҶ’          "searchTerms": "Pallof press band tutorial"
   360вҶ’        },
   361вҶ’        {
   362вҶ’          "id": "bird-dogs",
   363вҶ’          "name": "Bird Dogs",
   364вҶ’          "sets": "3Г—8 each side",
   365вҶ’          "notes": "On all fours, extend opposite arm and leg simultaneously. Hold top position for 2 seconds. Keep hips LEVEL вҖ” the most common mistake is letting the hip drop on the lifting side. Place a water bottle on your lower back as feedback вҖ” if it falls, you're rotating too much. This trains the cross-body stabilization pattern used in walking, running, and all unilateral exercises.",
   366вҶ’          "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=bird+dog+exercise+proper+form+tutorial",
   367вҶ’          "searchTerms": "Bird dog exercise proper form"
   368вҶ’        },
   369вҶ’        {
   370вҶ’          "id": "hollow-body-hold-d",
   371вҶ’          "name": "Hollow Body Hold",
   372вҶ’          "sets": "3Г—30 sec",
   373вҶ’          "notes": "Arms overhead, legs extended, low back pressed into floor, shoulders and feet lifted. Scale it: bent knees with arms at sides (easiest) \u2192 straight legs with arms at sides \u2192 straight legs with arms overhead (hardest). If you can hold 30s at the hardest version, you can progress to hollow body rocks.",
   374вҶ’          "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=hollow+body+hold+tutorial+progression+beginner",
   375вҶ’          "searchTerms": "Hollow body hold progression"
   376вҶ’        },
   377вҶ’        {
   378вҶ’          "id": "copenhagen-plank-d",
   379вҶ’          "name": "Copenhagen Plank",
   380вҶ’          "sets": "3Г—20-30 sec each side",
   381вҶ’          "notes": "Side plank with top leg on bench, bottom leg free. Trains lateral core and adductors simultaneously. Regression: bend bottom knee to floor, or use a shorter bench so you're supporting from the knee instead of the foot. Progression: add hip dips (lower and lift hips while holding). This exercise alone can fix a lot of your lateral movement issues.",
   382вҶ’          "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=copenhagen+plank+tutorial+regression+progression",
   383вҶ’          "searchTerms": "Copenhagen plank tutorial"
   384вҶ’        },
   385вҶ’        {
   386вҶ’          "id": "suitcase-carry",
   387вҶ’          "name": "Suitcase Carry",
   388вҶ’          "sets": "3Г—30 sec each side",
   389вҶ’          "notes": "Hold a single dumbbell at your side (moderate weight вҖ” 25-40 lb range), walk slowly. Keep your trunk PERFECTLY upright вҖ” don't lean away from the weight. This trains anti-lateral-flexion and grip strength simultaneously. You'll feel your obliques and deep stabilizers light up. Walk slowly and deliberately, don't rush.",
   390вҶ’          "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=suitcase+carry+exercise+anti+lateral+flexion",
   391вҶ’          "searchTerms": "Suitcase carry anti-lateral flexion"
   392вҶ’        }
   393вҶ’      ]
   394вҶ’    },
   395вҶ’    {
   396вҶ’      "id": "active-recovery",
   397вҶ’      "title": "Active Recovery",
   398вҶ’      "subtitle": "20-30 min вҖ” Do 1-2x per week",
   399вҶ’      "description": "Easy effort only. The purpose is blood flow, not fitness. If you find yourself breathing hard on the bike, you're going too hard. Pair with foam rolling to address tissue quality.",

400вҶ’      "sortOrder": 6,

   407вҶ’          "notes": "Conversational pace. RPE 3-4 out of 10. Slight resistance. This drives blood flow to your knees and ankles without impact stress. Good for Achilles flare-up days too.",

   414вҶ’          "sets": "2 min each leg",
   415вҶ’          "notes": "Slow passes, pause on tender spots for 30s. Focus on the outer quad / IT band area вҖ” this feeds into your lateral lunge knee issue.",

   424вҶ’          "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=foam+roll+calves+achilles+area",
   425вҶ’          "searchTerms": "Foam roll calves"
   426вҶ’        },
   427вҶ’        {
   428вҶ’          "id": "lacrosse-ball-shoulder",
   429вҶ’          "name": "Lacrosse Ball \u2014 Shoulder / Upper Back",
   430вҶ’          "sets": "2 min each side",
   431вҶ’          "notes": "Pin ball between your back and the wall. Roll around the scapula (shoulder blade) area and posterior deltoid. Find the spots that refer sensation вҖ” those are the adhesions limiting your shoulder mobility.",
   432вҶ’          "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=lacrosse+ball+shoulder+upper+back+release",
   433вҶ’          "searchTerms": "Lacrosse ball shoulder release"
   434вҶ’        }
   435вҶ’      ]
   436вҶ’    }
   437вҶ’  ],

   551вҶ’      "status": "current",
   552вҶ’      "summary": "This is the program you're in now. Rebuild shoulder stability, knee resilience, Achilles health, core strength, and thoracic mobility. No skill work yet \u2014 your joints aren't ready.",
   553вҶ’      "keyFocus": "The temptation will be to start skill work early. Don't. Every week of foundation you invest here saves you months of plateau and re-injury later. If you fail any gate test at week 8, extend Phase 1 by 2-week increments until you pass.",

        "Eccentric calf raises: full single-leg protocol",
        "Dead hangs: progress to active hangs (depress shoulders while hanging)",
        "Add 5-10s to all timed holds",
        "Core: Dead bugs with straight legs, hollow body with legs extended, Copenhagen plank full (20-30s), add Pallof press with medium band"
      ]
    },
    {
      "id": "load",
      "weekRange": "Weeks 5-8",
      "phaseName": "Load",
      "focus": "Start adding external load to mobility positions.",
      "weekStart": 5,
      "weekEnd": 8,
      "sortOrder": 3,
      "details": [
        "CARs: full effort with irradiation (squeeze fist, brace core)",
        "Goblet Cossack Squats with light dumbbell",
        "ATG Split Squat with light dumbbells, no support",
        "Weighted deep squat holds (goblet position)",
        "Y-T-W raises: increase dumbbell weight by 2-3 lb",
        "Begin testing overhead press and front squat with improved positions",
        "Single-leg balance on unstable surface (folded towel or pillow)",
        "Core: Hollow body rocks, Copenhagen plank with hip dips, suitcase carries at 30-40 lb, dead bugs with band resistance"
      ]
    }
  ],

  "roadmap": [
    {
      "id": "phase1",
      "name": "Phase 1: Joint Restoration",
      "weeks": "Weeks 1-8",
      "status": "current",
      "summary": "This is the program you're in now. Rebuild shoulder stability, knee resilience, Achilles health, core strength, and thoracic mobility. No skill work yet \u2014 your joints aren't ready.",
      "keyFocus": "The temptation will be to start skill work early. Don't. Every week of foundation you invest here saves you months of plateau and re-injury later. If you fail any gate test at week 8, extend Phase 1 by 2-week increments until you pass.",
      "gateTests": [
        "pain-free-planks",
        "strict-pullups-8",
        "clean-dips-15",
        "cossack-squat-full",
        "single-leg-balance-30",
        "wall-dorsiflexion-4in",

      ],
      "color": "rgb(96, 165, 224)",
      "sortOrder": 1
    },
    {
      "id": "phase2",
      "name": "Phase 2: Strength Prerequisites",
      "weeks": "Weeks 9-20 (12 weeks)",
      "status": "future",
      "summary": "Build the raw strength and positions your skills require. Shoulder strength, straight-arm conditioning, single-leg loading, and wrist preparation. Daily CARs and core work continue as maintenance.",
      "keyFocus": "This phase is dominated by pull-up volume, dip volume, and straight-arm conditioning. If you're doing BODi, use it for lower body days and replace upper body BODi with the skill-prep work. The false grip and straight-arm work will feel completely foreign \u2014 your tendons need time to adapt.",
      "gateTests": [

    {
      "id": "phase3",
      "name": "Phase 3: Skill Acquisition",
      "weeks": "Weeks 21-36 (16 weeks)",
      "status": "future",
      "summary": "Active skill practice begins. Muscle-up transition training, planche lean progressions, pistol squat depth development, and handstand balance work. This is as much neurological as physical.",
      "keyFocus": "Skill days replace some strength days. You're now practicing 3-4 skills per week alongside maintenance mobility and strength. The muscle-up will likely click before the planche \u2014 the transition is a technique breakthrough moment. Film every attempt.",
      "gateTests": [
        "muscle-ups-1-3",
        "tuck-planche-5s",
        "pistol-squat-full",
        "freestanding-handstand-20s",
        "negative-muscle-up-3s",

   624вҶ’  "skills": [

   627вҶ’      "name": "Muscle Up",
   628вҶ’      "priority": 1,
   629вҶ’      "icon": "\u2605",
   630вҶ’      "color": "rgb(224, 134, 96)",
   631вҶ’      "currentState": "3-5 pull-ups (previously 7-10 before shoulder issues). No muscle-up experience. Shoulder pain during planks and pressing.",
   632вҶ’      "requirements": "12+ strict pull-ups, 15+ dips, false grip strength, explosive pulling power, deep shoulder extension under load, and the transition technique.",
   633вҶ’      "gapAnalysis": "Your pulling strength base exists but is suppressed by shoulder instability. Once shoulders stabilize (Phase 1), pull-up numbers will rebound quickly since the muscle memory is there. The false grip and transition are completely new skills. The deep shoulder extension required at the transition point is the riskiest part given your shoulder history \u2014 this is why we must not rush.",
   634вҶ’      "timeline": "First muscle-up: 6-9 months (weeks 24-36). Confident reps: 9-12 months.",
   635вҶ’      "milestones": [
   636вҶ’        { "name": "8-10 strict pull-ups", "description": "Rebuild pulling base after shoulder stabilization", "targetWeek": 8 },
   637вҶ’        { "name": "12-15 strict pull-ups", "description": "Strength prerequisite for explosive pulling", "targetWeek": 20 },
   638вҶ’        { "name": "False grip hang 20+ sec", "description": "Wrist and forearm conditioning for transition", "targetWeek": 16 },
   639вҶ’        { "name": "High pull-ups to sternum", "description": "Explosive pulling height needed for transition", "targetWeek": 20 },
   640вҶ’        { "name": "Negative muscle-up (5 sec descent)", "description": "Eccentric control through the transition", "targetWeek": 24 },
   641вҶ’        { "name": "First muscle-up", "description": "Bar or rings, any style", "targetWeek": 30 },
   642вҶ’        { "name": "3-5 consecutive muscle-ups", "description": "Confident, repeatable reps", "targetWeek": 44 }

   652вҶ’      "requirements": "Strong scapular protraction, straight-arm pressing strength, progressive lean tolerance, conditioned wrists. The tuck planche is the most achievable planche variation and still looks impressive \u2014 hips level with shoulders, knees tucked to chest, body floating on hands.",
   653вҶ’      "gapAnalysis": "The tuck planche requires straight-arm pressing with protracted scapulae that standard lifting doesn't build. Your shoulders need to be fully stable before you start leaning. The good news: tuck planche is a realistic 9-15 month goal, well within the same timeline as your muscle-up. Advanced tuck (legs partially extended) is the stretch goal beyond that.",
   654вҶ’      "timeline": "Planche lean: 4-6 months. Frog stand: 3-4 months. Tuck planche (2-3 sec): 9-12 months. Tuck planche (5-10 sec): 12-15 months. Advanced tuck planche: 15-24 months (stretch goal).",
   655вҶ’      "milestones": [
   656вҶ’        { "name": "Pain-free plank 60+ sec", "description": "Baseline pressing endurance", "targetWeek": 8 },
   657вҶ’        { "name": "Frog stand hold", "description": "Balance and wrist conditioning", "targetWeek": 14 },
   658вҶ’        { "name": "Planche lean 20+ degrees", "description": "Progressive forward lean tolerance", "targetWeek": 20 },
   659вҶ’        { "name": "Tuck planche 2-3 sec", "description": "First hold with hips level", "targetWeek": 36 },
   660вҶ’        { "name": "Tuck planche 5-10 sec", "description": "Solid, repeatable hold", "targetWeek": 48 },
   661вҶ’        { "name": "Advanced tuck planche 3-5 sec", "description": "Legs partially extended", "targetWeek": 60 }
   662вҶ’      ]

   671вҶ’      "requirements": "Full ankle dorsiflexion (especially right side), strong VMO and quad, hip flexor strength to hold the non-working leg extended, single-leg balance, and hip mobility to sit deep without butt wink.",
   672вҶ’      "gapAnalysis": "Your three limiters are exactly what Phase 1 targets: ankle dorsiflexion, knee stability, and hip mobility. The specific gap is single-leg strength and hip flexor endurance. Your right side will always be harder due to the Achilles \u2014 accept asymmetry and work both sides to their respective maximums.",
   673вҶ’      "timeline": "Pistol to box (16 inch): 8-12 weeks. Pistol to box (8 inch): 16-20 weeks. Full-depth pistol (both legs): 5-8 months. Right leg may lag 2-4 weeks behind left.",

   691вҶ’      "gapAnalysis": "Your t-spine restriction and shoulder instability are the current blockers. Once those resolve (Phase 1-2), the handstand becomes primarily a balance skill. At 46, balance adaptation is slower but absolutely happens \u2014 expect more time in wall-supported positions. Your core strength from Session D will transfer directly.",
   692вҶ’      "timeline": "Wall handstand 30 sec: 3-4 months. Wall handstand 60 sec with controlled entry: 5-6 months. Freestanding 5-10 sec: 6-9 months. Freestanding 15-30 sec: 9-15 months.",

   695вҶ’        { "name": "Wall handstand 30 sec", "description": "Stomach-to-wall hold", "targetWeek": 14 },

   706вҶ’      "name": "Wall Dorsiflexion Test",

   708вҶ’      "target": "Left: 4-5 inches. Right (Achilles): will start lower \u2014 track improvement, don't compare sides.",
   709вҶ’      "frequency": "Test day 1, then every 2 weeks",
   710вҶ’      "unit": "inches"
   711вҶ’    },
   712вҶ’    {

   714вҶ’      "name": "Single-Leg Balance (Eyes Closed)",
   715вҶ’      "howTo": "Stand on one foot, close eyes, time how long before you touch down or open eyes.",
   716вҶ’      "target": "30 seconds each side. Right ankle will lag \u2014 that's expected.",
   717вҶ’      "frequency": "Test day 1, then every 2 weeks",
   718вҶ’      "unit": "seconds"

   722вҶ’      "name": "Hollow Body Hold Time",
   723вҶ’      "howTo": "Full position (arms overhead, legs straight). Time until low back lifts off floor.",
   724вҶ’      "target": "Week 1: 10-15s is normal. Week 8 goal: 30-45s.",
   725вҶ’      "frequency": "Test day 1, then every 3 weeks",
   726вҶ’      "unit": "seconds"
   727вҶ’    },
   728вҶ’    {
   729вҶ’      "id": "overhead-reach-test",
   730вҶ’      "name": "Overhead Reach (Wall Test)",
   731вҶ’      "howTo": "Stand with back, butt, and head against wall. Raise arms overhead trying to touch thumbs to wall without arching your low back off the wall.",
   732вҶ’      "target": "Thumbs touch wall with back flat = good t-spine and shoulder mobility. If back arches, that's your t-spine restriction.",
   733вҶ’      "frequency": "Test day 1, then every 3 weeks"
   734вҶ’    },
   735вҶ’    {
   736вҶ’      "id": "deep-squat-test",
   737вҶ’      "name": "Deep Squat Assessment",
   738вҶ’      "howTo": "Feet shoulder width, squat as deep as possible without any support. Note: heels lifting, excessive forward lean, knees caving, inability to keep chest up.",
   739вҶ’      "target": "Hips below parallel, heels down, chest up, no pain. Use a phone to film yourself \u2014 you'll miss compensations otherwise.",
   740вҶ’      "frequency": "Test day 1, film it. Re-film every 4 weeks."
   741вҶ’    },
   742вҶ’    {
   743вҶ’      "id": "ninety-ninety-hip-test",
   744вҶ’      "name": "90/90 Hip Test",
   745вҶ’      "howTo": "Sit in 90/90 position (both legs at 90\u00b0). Can you sit tall with both knees touching or near the floor?",
   746вҶ’      "target": "Both knees within 3-4 inches of floor, spine upright without hands for support.",
   747вҶ’      "frequency": "Test day 1, then every 3 weeks"
   748вҶ’    },
   749вҶ’    {
   750вҶ’      "id": "cross-body-shoulder-reach",
   751вҶ’      "name": "Cross-Body Shoulder Reach",
   752вҶ’      "howTo": "Reach right hand behind your back from below, left hand from above. How close can your fingertips get? Repeat on the other side.",
   753вҶ’      "target": "Fingers touching or overlapping. Note the difference between sides \u2014 your tighter side is the priority.",
   754вҶ’      "frequency": "Test day 1, then every 4 weeks"
   755вҶ’    },
   756вҶ’    {
   757вҶ’      "id": "pain-free-planks",
   758вҶ’      "name": "Pain-Free Planks",
   759вҶ’      "howTo": "Hold a standard plank position. Note any shoulder pain during or after.",
   760вҶ’      "target": "45+ seconds with zero shoulder pain."
   761вҶ’    },
   762вҶ’    {

   766вҶ’      "target": "8-10 strict pull-ups."

   772вҶ’      "target": "15+ clean dips."
   773вҶ’    },

   777вҶ’      "howTo": "Full-depth bodyweight Cossack squat each side, no external support.",
   778вҶ’      "target": "Full depth both sides, no support, no pain."

   784вҶ’      "target": "30 seconds each side, eyes closed.",
   785вҶ’      "unit": "seconds"
   786вҶ’    },
   787вҶ’    {
   788вҶ’      "id": "wall-dorsiflexion-4in",
   789вҶ’      "name": "Wall Dorsiflexion 4+ Inches",
   790вҶ’      "howTo": "Face wall, drive knee forward. Measure distance from wall where heel stays down.",
   791вҶ’      "target": "4+ inches each side.",
   792вҶ’      "unit": "inches"

   797вҶ’      "howTo": "Arms overhead, legs straight. Hold until low back lifts off floor.",

You are working in the worktree at `/Users/ryanr/Code/hevy-planner/.worktrees/v1-implementation`.

The types have been updated. Key changes:
- `ExerciseMappingRow` вҶ’ `ExerciseTemplateMappingRow` (fields: user_id, program_template_id, hevy_template_id, is_custom)
- New `RoutineMappingRow` (fields: user_id, program_routine_id, hevy_routine_id)
- `QueueItemRow.session_id` вҶ’ `routine_id`

### 1. Update `src/storage/queries.ts`

Read the file first, then make these changes:

- Import `ExerciseTemplateMappingRow, RoutineMappingRow` instead of `ExerciseMappingRow`
- `insertQueueItems`: change `session_id` to `routine_id` in both the SQL and the items array type
- `getExerciseMappings` вҶ’ `getExerciseTemplateMappings`: query `exercise_template_mappings` table, return `ExerciseTemplateMappingRow[]`
- `upsertExerciseMapping` вҶ’ `upsertExerciseTemplateMapping`: upsert into `exercise_template_mappings` with columns `user_id, program_template_id, hevy_template_id, is_custom`, conflict on `(user_id, program_template_id)`
- Add `getRoutineMappings(db, userId)`: query `routine_mappings` table, return `RoutineMappingRow[]`
- Add `upsertRoutineMapping(db, mapping: RoutineMappingRow)`: upsert into `routine_mappings`, conflict on `(user_id, program_routine_id)`
- Add `bulkSetQueueItemRoutineIds(db, userId, routineIdMap: Map<string, string>)`: for each entry in the map (program_routine_id вҶ’ hevy_routine_id), update queue_items SET hevy_routine_id WHERE user_id AND routine_id matches. Use db.batch for efficiency.

### 2. Create migration file at `migrations/0002_exercise_template_mappings.sql`

First check if a `migrations/` directory exists. If not, create it. The migration should:

```sql
-- Drop old table
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS exercise_mappings;

-- New exercise template mappings
CREATE TABLE exercise_template_mappings (
  user_id TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES users(id),
  program_template_id TEXT NOT NULL,
  hevy_template_id TEXT NOT NULL,
  is_custom INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
  PRIMARY KEY (user_id, program_template_id)
);

-- Routine mappings
CREATE TABLE routine_mappings (
  user_id TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES users(id),
  program_routine_id TEXT NOT NULL,
  hevy_routine_id TEXT NOT NULL,
  PRIMARY KEY (user_id, program_routine_id)
);

-- Rename session_id column in queue_items to routine_id
-- SQLite doesn't support ALTER COLUMN RENAME, so recreate the table
CREATE TABLE queue_items_new (
  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT,
  user_id TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES users(id),
  routine_id TEXT NOT NULL,
  position INTEGER NOT NULL,
  status TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT 'pending',
  completed_date TEXT,
  hevy_routine_id TEXT,
  hevy_workout_id TEXT
);

INSERT INTO queue_items_new (id, user_id, routine_id, position, status, completed_date, hevy_routine_id, hevy_workout_id)
SELECT id, user_id, session_id, position, status, completed_date, hevy_routine_id, hevy_workout_id
FROM queue_items;

DROP TABLE queue_items;
ALTER TABLE queue_items_new RENAME TO queue_items;
```

Also check and update `migrations/0001_initial.sql` if it exists вҖ” update `session_id` вҶ’ `routine_id` and `exercise_mappings` вҶ’ `exercise_template_mappings` in the initial schema to match. Read it first to see the current schema.

The types have been updated. Key changes:
- `ExerciseMappingRow` вҶ’ `ExerciseTemplateMappingRow` (fields: user_id, program_template_id, hevy_template_id, is_custom)
- New `RoutineMappingRow` (fields: user_id, program_routine_id, hevy_routine_id)
- `QueueItemRow.session_id` вҶ’ `routine_id`

### 1. Update `src/storage/queries.ts`

Read the file first, then make these changes:

- Import `ExerciseTemplateMappingRow, RoutineMappingRow` instead of `ExerciseMappingRow`
- `insertQueueItems`: change `session_id` to `routine_id` in both the SQL and the items array type
- `getExerciseMappings` вҶ’ `getExerciseTemplateMappings`: query `exercise_template_mappings` table, return `ExerciseTemplateMappingRow[]`
- `upsertExerciseMapping` вҶ’ `upsertExerciseTemplateMapping`: upsert into `exercise_template_mappings` with columns `user_id, program_template_id, hevy_template_id, is_custom`, conflict on `(user_id, program_template_id)`
- Add `getRoutineMappings(db, userId)`: query `routine_mappings` table, return `RoutineMappingRow[]`
- Add `upsertRoutineMapping(db, mapping: RoutineMappingRow)`: upsert into `routine_mappings`, conflict on `(user_id, program_routine_id)`
- Add `bulkSetQueueItemRoutineIds(db, userId, routineIdMap: Map<string, string>)`: for each entry in the map (program_routine_id вҶ’ hevy_routine_id), update queue_items SET hevy_routine_id WHERE user_id AND routine_id matches. Use db.batch for efficiency.

### 2. Create migration file at `migrations/0002_exercise_template_mappings.sql`

First check if a `migrations/` directory exists. If not, create it. The migration should:

```sql
-- Drop old table
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS exercise_mappings;

-- New exercise template mappings
CREATE TABLE exercise_template_mappings (
  user_id TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES users(id),
  program_template_id TEXT NOT NULL,
  hevy_template_id TEXT NOT NULL,
  is_custom INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
  PRIMARY KEY (user_id, program_template_id)
);

-- Routine mappings
CREATE TABLE routine_mappings (
  user_id TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES users(id),
  program_routine_id TEXT NOT NULL,
  hevy_routine_id TEXT NOT NULL,
  PRIMARY KEY (user_id, program_routine_id)
);

-- Rename session_id column in queue_items to routine_id
-- SQLite doesn't support ALTER COLUMN RENAME, so recreate the table
CREATE TABLE queue_items_new (
  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT,
  user_id TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES users(id),
  routine_id TEXT NOT NULL,
  position INTEGER NOT NULL,
  status TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT 'pending',
  completed_date TEXT,
  hevy_routine_id TEXT,
  hevy_workout_id TEXT
);

INSERT INTO queue_items_new (id, user_id, routine_id, position, status, completed_date, hevy_routine_id, hevy_workout_id)
SELECT id, user_id, session_id, position, status, completed_date, hevy_routine_id, hevy_workout_id
FROM queue_items;

DROP TABLE queue_items;
ALTER TABLE queue_items_new RENAME TO queue_items;
```

Also check and update `migrations/0001_initial.sql` if it exists вҖ” update `session_id` вҶ’ `routine_id` and `exercise_mappings` вҶ’ `exercise_template_mappings` in the initial schema to match. Read it first to see the current schema.

  "benchmarks": [
    {
      "id": "wall-dorsiflexion",
      "name": "Wall Dorsiflexion Test",
      "howTo": "Face wall, foot back from wall. Drive knee forward keeping heel down. Measure max distance (inches) where knee touches wall and heel stays down.",
      "target": "Left: 4-5 inches. Right (Achilles): will start lower вҖ” track improvement, don't compare sides.",
      "frequency": "Test day 1, then every 2 weeks",
      "unit": "inches"
    },
    {
      "id": "single-leg-balance-test",
      "name": "Single-Leg Balance (Eyes Closed)",
      "howTo": "Stand on one foot, close eyes, time how long before you touch down or open eyes.",
      "target": "30 seconds each side. Right ankle will lag вҖ” that's expected.",
      "frequency": "Test day 1, then every 2 weeks",
      "unit": "seconds"
    },
    {
      "id": "hollow-body-hold-test",
      "name": "Hollow Body Hold Time",
      "howTo": "Full position (arms overhead, legs straight). Time until low back lifts off floor.",
      "target": "Week 1: 10-15s is normal. Week 8 goal: 30-45s.",
      "frequency": "Test day 1, then every 3 weeks",
      "unit": "seconds"
    },
    {
      "id": "overhead-reach-test",
      "name": "Overhead Reach (Wall Test)",
      "howTo": "Stand with back, butt, and head against wall. Raise arms overhead trying to touch thumbs to wall without arching your low back off the wall.",
      "target": "Thumbs touch wall with back flat = good t-spine and shoulder mobility. If back arches, that's your t-spine restriction.",
      "frequency": "Test day 1, then every 3 weeks"
    },
    {
      "id": "deep-squat-test",
      "name": "Deep Squat Assessment",
      "howTo": "Feet shoulder width, squat as deep as possible without any support. Note: heels lifting, excessive forward lean, knees caving, inability to keep chest up.",
      "target": "Hips below parallel, heels down, chest up, no pain. Use a phone to film yourself вҖ” you'll miss compensations otherwise.",
      "frequency": "Test day 1, film it. Re-film every 4 weeks."
    },
    {
      "id": "ninety-ninety-hip-test",
      "name": "90/90 Hip Test",
      "howTo": "Sit in 90/90 position (both legs at 90В°). Can you sit tall with both knees touching or near the floor?",
      "target": "Both knees within 3-4 inches of floor, spine upright without hands for support.",
      "frequency": "Test day 1, then every 3 weeks"
    },
    {
      "id": "cross-body-shoulder-reach",
      "name": "Cross-Body Shoulder Reach",
      "howTo": "Reach right hand behind your back from below, left hand from above. How close can your fingertips get? Repeat on the other side.",
      "target": "Fingers touching or overlapping. Note the difference between sides вҖ” your tighter side is the priority.",
      "frequency": "Test day 1, then every 4 weeks"
    },
    {
      "id": "pain-free-planks",
      "name": "Pain-Free Planks",
      "howTo": "Hold a standard plank position. Note any shoulder pain during or after.",
      "target": "45+ seconds with zero shoulder pain."
    },
    {
      "id": "strict-pullups-8",
      "name": "Strict Pull-ups (Phase 1 Gate)",
      "howTo": "Dead hang start, pull chin over bar, full lockout between reps. No kipping.",
      "target": "8-10 strict pull-ups."
    },
    {
      "id": "clean-dips-15",
      "name": "Clean Dips (Phase 1 Gate)",
      "howTo": "Parallel bars or bench dips, full range of motion, controlled tempo.",
      "target": "15+ clean dips."
    },
    {
      "id": "cossack-squat-full",
